---------------
TECHNICAL NOTES
---------------
This patch is an unofficial bug fix for several bugs that mainly occur in chapter 22 of Dark Deity.  It is my hope that eventually these bugs will be fixed in an official game release, so I am documenting the technical nature of the changes I made and my investigation into them so the nature of bugs and the fixes can be understood by others attempting to refine or implement such bug fixes.

if someone wants to understand move quickly the exact nature of the changes made between the pre-existing code and the changed script code, all you have to do is use the patch installation tool to install the patch then note that when it installs the scripts located in the scripts folder it also extracts the pre-existing scripts into the originals folder, so right after installing the patch you can use a differencing tool like WinMerge to easily compare the new nad old code side by side.


There are two types of crashes that frequently occur in chapter 22 in the release version of the game which I made workarounds for.

Crash type 1 - Variable passable not set on an object before reading it
Example crash dump:
___________________________________________
############################################################################################
ERROR in
action number 1
of Step Event0
for object obj_ai_controller:

Variable <unknown_object>.passable(100144, -2147483648) not set before reading it.
at gml_Script_scr_movement_range_ai
############################################################################################
gml_Script_scr_movement_range_ai (line -1)
gml_Object_obj_ai_controller_Step_0


This crash dump indicates that the code tries to access the passable variable but it doesn't exist yet on some object.  Using a grep tool on the decompiled script code dumps it appears to me that the only objects that have the passable variable are obj_node objects.  passable is only checked in two lines of code in all of scr_movement_range_ai  One of those accesses current.passable and the other accesses neighbor.passable.  These are obtained by iterating over the neighbors list with some processing in and out of an open and a closed priority queue to keep track of which nodes have been used and are no longer open for a unit to move to.  It seemed to me like these two variables should be only populated with obj_node objects since those are the only objects that have the passable variable, so I investigated how those get initialized.  Obj_node initializes the passable variable right in obj_node's Create event obj_node_Create_0 so there's no way that any obj_node that was ever created should be missing the passable variable, so my analysis of that is that it must be that the object in current or neighbor was not an obj_node object as expected.

I made a function (scr_debug_assert_instance_type(instance, expected_object_index (type of the object), location (a string that I used pass the name of a checkpoint or the name of a variable to display in the event of an invalid valud detected so I could identify which one of the callers of this function were responsible)).  The function has a var debug defined in its body that if it is true will allow it to pop up a modal dialog window to report the error and if it is false it will not.  Either way the function will have the result true if the assertion passes and all is as expected and false if it does not pass and the object instance is not of the type that was being asserted or the object instance doesn't exist.  I called this function in the two places in scr_movement_range_ai where I replaced an existing check of if (instance_exists({variable})) with if (instance_exists({variable}) && scr_debug_assert_instance_type({variable}, obj_node, "movement_range_ai:{variable}") to prevent the node from being processed if the instance isn't actually a node as well as if it isn't an instance and that made the crash bug related to passable in scr_movement_range_ai go away.  I detected in my debugging two other types that could end up in neighbor rarely, and those are the cause of this type of crash.  One type was obj_cursor_follower, and the other was not actually a single object type but it was some object that inherits from of obj_actor like obj_enemy_delian_ranger or obj_enemy_delian_knight.  I have tried to figure out where these get introduced in the code without any success.  The obj_cursor_follower object in particular befuddles me because there's not even any script I could locate that uses obj_cursor_follower except in obj_cursor_Create_0, the create event for obj_cursor, but it creates an instance of cursor_follower and doesn't even assign it to any variable that can be used to reference it.  I couldn't find any place in the code that uses the obj_cursor_follower type such as with the statement with(obj_cursor_follower) { } to iterate over objects of that instance type without the use of a stored variable of that type, so I don't have a clue how an obj_cursor_follower could ever end up assigned to the list of neighbors that neighbor iterates over.

Because I couldn't figure out where obj_cursor_follower somehow gets assigned to neighbor, I couldn't really fix the original root cause of the passable bug but I did at least find a way to prevent it from crashing the game by the debug assertions that force it the code to ignore any neighbor or current objects that aren't actually obj_node in scr_movement_range_ai.  After doing that I found through further testing that this bug also definitively occurs in scr_movement_range and scr_movement_range_hover as well which I fixed in the same way as movement_range_ai.  Using a grep tool on the script code I also found that passable is checked in an identical fashion in three other scr_movement_range functions: scr_movement_range_zone, scr_movement_range_ai_dragon, and scr_movement_range_hover_dragon, so I also patched those in the same way as the first 3 in order to prevent the same bug from potentially crashing them.

All total the mitigations for this bug involved implementing a new function
scr_debug_assert_instance_type
and placing 2 debug assertions (one for current and one for neighbor) in each of the 6 scr_movement_range files:
scr_movement_range
scr_movement_range_ai
scr_movement_range_ai_dragon
scr_movement_range_hover
scr_movement_range_hover_dragon
scr_movement_range_zone


Crash type 2 - Data structure with index does not exist

Example crash dump:
___________________________________________
############################################################################################
ERROR in
action number 1
of Step Event0
for object obj_cursor:

Data structure with index does not exist.
at gml_Script_scr_movement_range_hover
############################################################################################
gml_Script_scr_movement_range_hover (line -1)
gml_Object_obj_cursor_Step_0

Its a bit difficult to locate the exact code in scr_movement_range that causes an error like this because you can see it doesn't provide the name of the data structure with the index that does not exist.  It has to be some kind of structure that is normally accessed by index such as a ds_list or a ds_grid, but there are quite a few places where the code in scr_movement_range_hover reads values from ds_list structures via the ds_list_find_value() so to pinpoint the location of the bug is guesswork without some way to narrow it down.  To do that I created three new functions scr_set_attackdisplay_range1(node), scr_set_attackdisplay_range2(node), scr_set_attackdisplay_range3 because in the code there are three distinct sections where it performs a lot of ds_list_find_value operations to locate nodes and set the attackdisplay variable on them to true to paint the nodes at a certain distance away from the current node red on screen to indicate its possible to attack when the game is displaying a movement range visually on screen.  I moved the code from each section into one of those functions so that when the crash occurred I could figure out if it was one of those sections in particular causing the error. After a good bit of testing in this new code configuration all of the crashes I detected as far as I can remember were caused by the range3 section, which only applies to sniper characters.  This explains why many players have reported that not using sniper characters helped them get through chapter 22.  Every access of an index in this section is related to ds_list_find_value on either viewneighborsranged or viewneighborsmelee.  Examining the code in this section I found that it iterates over array index 0 through 3 of current.viewneighborsmelee then for each of those it iterates over array index 0 through 7 of the viewneighborsranged of each of those nodes.  Since the error says that the index does not exist as reported in the crash dump, it seems that either viewneighborsmelee has less than 4 entries in its list or viewneighborsranged has less than 8 items in its list once in awhile so trying to access them crashes the game.

To fix this problem I rewrote the code in scr_set_attackdisplay_range3 so that instead of accessing a fixed number of indices with hardcoded values it would use a for loop and iterate only over whatever number of entries actually exist in viewneighborsmelee and viewneighborsranged using a for loop with ds_list_size() as the maximum limit and it seemed like the crashes had gone away.  I did some more grep searching to find all the code in the game that accesses viewneighborsranged or viewneighborsmelee with a fixed number of entries instead of iterating over ds_list_size() to try to resolve any other potential crashes related to the same issue, even though I did not actually experience any in my limited testing.  This led to me rewriting scr_set_attacksiplay_range1() and scr_set_attackdisplay_range2() with for loops and the debug assertion to limit operation to only on obj_node instances the same way as I had scr_set_attackdisplay_range3() as a precautionary measure although I don't know if that was necessary because I don't remember experiencing a crash in those. I also found code related to healers in obj_ai_controller_Step_0 and obj_purpleai_controller_Step_0 that analyzed a fixed number of nodes of viewneighborsranged and viewneighborsmelee so I rewrote those as for loops as well.

At this point I thought the changes to the way the game iterates over viewneighborsranged and viewneighbrsmelee had stopped all the crashes but after some more testing, playing through chapter 22 around 10 or more times I found that once in awhile, maybe only one time per playthrough of chapter 22 or even one time in three, I would still receive a crash in this section related to an index that does not exist.  This had me stumped but due to my experience with the wrong type of node being received in the code related to the neighbors list while resolving crash type #1 and viewneigborsranged and viewneighborsmelee being derived ultimately from neighbors (in obj_game_Create_0) I decided to also put the scr_debug_assert_instance_type check right at the start of scr_set_attacksiplay_range3 to filter out anything that wasn't an instance of type obj_node.  That seems to have stopped any remaining crashes due to invalid indexes.  I haven't received any crashes in at least 5 tries to play through chapter 22 so I feel like this has solved the remaining issue.  I still remain in the dark about the ultimate source of invalid types in neighbor lists but the mitigations I have implemented and described here seem to prevent them from bringing down the game.


Other than code changes that directly are related to avoiding crash type #1 and crash type #2 in chapter 22, I implemented two other changes that might not have been strictly necessary but which address other minor bugs or inconsistencies I came across.  As I said before there are times when the number of viewneighborsranged items for sure is less than 8 and that definitely caused crashing in the code that I isolated in scr_attackdisplay_range3.  I tried to figure out why that is and that led me to discover that there are two places that viewneighborsranged are set in objects.  One of those is in scr_obj_game_Create_0 and the other is in scr_obj_game_cutscene_Create_0.  In obj_game at the end of setting up the viewneighborsranged list and viewneighborsmelee list on a node it checked to see if the number of nodes was less than 8 for viewneighborsranged or less than 4 for viewneighborsmelee and if it was then it added 8 or 4 copies of the last node it had added, as a measure to try to ensure that there were at least 8 or 4 items in the list to try to resolve crash bug #2.  This code should actually create some viewneighborsmelee lists of nodes at the edge of the map with 7 nodes instead of 3 in them and some viewneighborsranged lists with 13-15 entries in them instead of 5-7.  I'm guessing this code must have been put in by someone trying to resolve the invalid index problem that caused crash #2, but since I made all the accesses of viewneighborsranged and viewneighborsmelee indices in the game iterate over the actual size of the list instead of checking a hardcoded amount of exactly 4 or 8 values, there's no reason to need to add excess values to those lists as a clunky workaround to prevent checking a non-existing nod.  I wasn't sure if those excess entries that were added might be causing problems so I removed them as a preventive measure. I've looked at all the code in the game related to viewneighborsranged and viewneighbrosmelee and I see no need for them. obj_game_cutscene populates lists of viewneighborsranged and viewneighborsmelee as well, but it does not add excess entries like that.

The last change I made was as a result of noticing while I was playing through chapter 22 dozens of times that reinforcements that spawn every other round on the right end of the map could stack up.  Two of the enemies run towards you after spawning no matter how close to you they are and the other two stay where they spawn if you don't get close enough to them.  If you wait a long time to approach the ones that don't move you can be surprised when suddenly you find out when they start moving that there are three or four enemies stacked there, not just one.  I stumbled upon the code that causes this monster spawning in obj_turn_scripter_22_Other_10 which I guess is the event that decides every turn if any enemies should spawn while I was looking for something else.  I found that the game actually is meant to check and make sure they don't spawn if the node they are supposed to spawn on still has an enemy in it, but the code to spawn the unit instance uses x and y values which are one pixel each and the code that checks to see if the node is already occupied uses GridX, GridY.  GridX is suposed to be equivalent to 32 * GridX and y to 32 * GridY but at some point someone changed one of the two there without realizing they needed to change the other or they did some faulty mental math with the result that the code was checking one node to make sure it was empty before spawing the enemy unit on a completely different node.  Clearly the code written there was originally intended to avoid spawning a second, third, or fourth unit on a node that was already occupied, so I in obj_turn_scripter_22_Other_10 I made code modifications to correct all four node test values so they are checking the same nodes as the ones that the four units are spawning on.  If that was not the original intention of the code there would have never been a check to see if the nodes were empty first at all and there would have just been the instance spawning code without any empty node test conditional first.
